Peribronchial lymph nodes located at the foot of the B3 bronchus are very frequent and must be removed both for oncological reasons and to facilitate bronchial dissection.

This video clip illustrates the usual pattern of bronchi and arteries on the right upper lobe segments after opening the fissure.
More occasionally, a large vein in the fissure drains both the upper lobe and lower lobe segments.

The precise determination of the number of arteries by preoperative modeling is a precious help throughout the procedure.

On the left, the lingular vein is of normal diameter, whereas on the right, it is very thin, exposing to lingular ischemia after a left upper trisegmentectomy.
This complication has often been reported.
Therefore, in this case, it is preferable to respect this V3 vein during a left upper trisegmentectomy.

Note that the artery to segment 7 may be missing in 16% of the cases.
On the right lung, it is necessary to remember that a middle lobe artery may arise at a very low level of the basilar arterial trunk.
This is why, unless there is a special case, it is advisable to ligate or clip the segmental vein within the parenchyma and not at the level of the inferior pulmonary vein.
Such a case is demonstrated in this video with the control of V8.
The vein of segment 6 poses a particular problem.
In many cases, the most superior branch of the inferior pulmonary vein does not correspond to the V6 vein but to a common trunk between V6 and the basilar vein.
The level of section of the V6 vein must therefore be pushed further.
